skill.name: deploying-kafka-k8s
skill.description: Deploy Apache Kafka on Kubernetes with the Strimzi operator in KRaft mode. Use it for operator setup, Kafka cluster manifests, topic management, and producer or consumer testing.
skill.summary: Guide Strimzi-based Apache Kafka deployments on Kubernetes with KRaft mode.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static high-severity weak cryptography matches are false positives caused by ordinary documentation text such as KRaft and describe. The skill is legitimate Kubernetes deployment guidance, but it includes mutable remote manifest URLs and example Kafka configurations with plaintext or unauthenticated access that need publication warnings.

medium_findings.title: Mutable Remote Kubernetes Manifests
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to create, apply, or delete Kubernetes resources directly from Strimzi latest URLs. This is a legitimate operator installation pattern, but mutable remote manifests create supply chain and reproducibility risk if copied into production.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network commands and kubectl apply steps are explicit. The risk is contextual because the URLs are official Strimzi documentation endpoints, not suspicious domains.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext and Unauthenticated Kafka Examples
medium_findings.description: The production and integration examples include plaintext listeners, an external NodePort without TLS, and a Dapr component with authType set to none. These examples may be acceptable for demonstrations, but they are unsafe defaults for production Kafka traffic.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The YAML explicitly sets tls to false on listeners and authType to none. The surrounding content labels one cluster as production, increasing confidence that users could copy unsafe settings.

medium_findings.title: Cluster-Modifying Commands Require Operator Review
medium_findings.description: The skill includes kubectl commands that create namespaces, install operators, deploy clusters, run pods, and delete Kafka resources and PVCs. These commands match the skill purpose but can alter or destroy Kubernetes resources if run against the wrong context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly visible and operationally impactful. They are not malicious because they are documented deployment and cleanup steps for the advertised task.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography alerts appear to match documentation words and command text rather than cryptographic APIs or algorithms. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptography being used by executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found only prose, YAML, kubectl describe commands, and a local string check. None of the cited locations performs cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: False Positive System Reconnaissance Match
low_findings.description: The verification script reads SKILL.md and checks for required strings. No evidence found of host enumeration, credential discovery, network scanning, or system information collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited script uses local file reads and simple string membership checks only. It exits with status codes based on missing documentation content.

low_findings.title: Documentation Shell Blocks Are Not Auto-Executed
low_findings.description: The static external command alerts come from fenced shell examples in SKILL.md. They are expected for a Kubernetes deployment skill and are not executed automatically by the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear inside Markdown code fences and align with the stated deployment workflow. Risk remains only if users run them without reviewing context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Manifest Applied to Kubernetes
dangerous_patterns.description: kubectl commands reference remote latest URLs, which can change over time and should be pinned before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are explicit and use latest endpoints. The domain is the expected Strimzi site, so the issue is supply chain hygiene rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Kafka Listener Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: Example Kafka listener configuration sets tls to false, including on an external NodePort listener.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listener settings are explicit in the YAML. The risk is clear when used beyond isolated development environments.

content.user_title: Deploy Kafka on Kubernetes with Strimzi
content.value_statement: Running Kafka on Kubernetes requires coordinated operator, cluster, topic, and testing steps. This skill provides Strimzi-based guidance for KRaft Kafka deployments.
actual_capabilities: Guides Strimzi operator installation for Kubernetes Kafka clusters.
Provides single-node and three-node Kafka custom resource examples.
Shows KafkaTopic creation and topic inspection workflows.
Includes producer and consumer testing with Strimzi Kafka images.
Covers basic status checks, logs, events, and cleanup commands.
Includes a Dapr pub/sub component example for Kafka integration.
limitations: Does not cover managed Kafka services such as Confluent Cloud or Amazon MSK.
Requires an existing Kubernetes cluster, kubectl access, and suitable permissions.
Example manifests need hardening before production use.
Remote latest URLs should be pinned and reviewed before use.

use_cases.title: Create a development Kafka cluster
use_cases.description: Deploy a small Strimzi Kafka cluster for service integration testing on Kubernetes.

use_cases.title: Prepare a production Kafka baseline
use_cases.description: Adapt the three-node KRaft example with persistent storage, replicas, and monitoring settings.

use_cases.title: Validate Kafka operations
use_cases.description: Create topics, run producer and consumer checks, and inspect cluster health during rollout.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a development cluster
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a disposable Kubernetes environment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to deploy a single-node Strimzi Kafka cluster in my kafka namespace. Explain each step before I run it.

prompt_templates.title: Create and test a topic
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic topic management and message flow validation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a Kafka topic for order events and test it with console producer and consumer commands.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a production configuration
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate deployment planning for persistent Kafka workloads.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to adapt the three-node KRaft example for production with persistent storage, resource limits, and monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Review deployment security
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before sharing manifests with a platform team.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Strimzi Kafka deployment plan for TLS, authentication, storage, cleanup risk, and pinned manifest versions.

output_examples.input: Deploy a local Kafka cluster for integration testing.
output_examples.output: A namespace setup plan, Strimzi operator installation steps, a single-node Kafka cluster outline, and readiness checks.

output_examples.input: Create a topic and confirm messages can flow.
output_examples.output: A KafkaTopic creation workflow, topic listing guidance, and producer and consumer validation steps.

output_examples.input: Prepare this for a production review.
output: Storage and replica settings to verify.
TLS and authentication items to harden.

best_practices: Pin Strimzi manifests and container versions before production deployment.
Use TLS, authentication, and network policies for Kafka traffic.
Confirm the active kubectl context before running create, apply, or delete commands.
anti_patterns: Do not expose Kafka externally with plaintext listeners in production.
Do not run cleanup commands until PVC retention and data loss impact are reviewed.
Do not apply remote latest manifests without reviewing their contents.

faq.question: Does this skill install Kafka automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and manifests that a user can review and run in Kubernetes.

faq.question: Which Kafka operator does it use?
faq.answer: It uses the Strimzi operator and examples for Kafka in KRaft mode.

faq.question: Is it suitable for managed Kafka?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on Kubernetes-hosted Kafka, not managed services such as MSK or Confluent Cloud.

faq.question: Can it help with topic management?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes KafkaTopic examples and commands to list or describe topics.

faq.question: Does it include production security hardening?
faq.answer: Only partially. Users should add TLS, authentication, authorization, and reviewed network exposure settings.

faq.question: What should be checked before running the commands?
faq.answer: Check the kubectl context, namespace, manifest version, storage settings, and cleanup impact.
